agent_output: {"executive_summary":"Orchestration-based saga for a 4-step courier marketplace order flow (Order → Inventory → Payment → Courier). A dedicated Saga Orchestrator drives the happy path forward and, on any step failure, executes compensating transactions in reverse order. This avoids distributed transactions while guaranteeing eventual consistency across all four services.","architecture":{"components":[{"name":"Saga Orchestrator","responsibility":"Owns the saga state machine. Drives forward steps, detects failures, triggers compensations in reverse order. Persists saga state to survive crashes.","boundaries":"Knows step ordering and compensation mapping. Does NOT contain business logic—delegates to each service."},{"name":"Order Service","responsibility":"Creates order records (PENDING), confirms orders (CONFIRMED), cancels orders (CANCELLED). Owns the Order aggregate.","boundaries":"Order lifecycle only. No knowledge of inventory, payment, or courier concerns."},{"name":"Inventory Service","responsibility":"Reserves inventory for order items, releases reservations on compensation. Owns the Reservation aggregate.","boundaries":"Stock management only. Idempotent reserve/release keyed by orderId."},{"name":"Payment Service","responsibility":"Charges customer payment method, refunds on compensation. Owns the Payment aggregate.","boundaries":"Payment processing only. Idempotent charge/refund keyed by orderId."},{"name":"Courier Service","responsibility":"Assigns an available courier to the order, unassigns on compensation. Owns the Assignment aggregate.","boundaries":"Courier assignment only. Idempotent assign/unassign keyed by orderId."},{"name":"Saga State Store","responsibility":"Durable persistence of saga instance state (current step, status, compensation log). Enables crash recovery.","boundaries":"Infrastructure concern. Could be a dedicated table or document collection."}],"interfaces":[{"from":"Saga Orchestrator","to":"Order Service","contract":"createOrder(orderId, items, customerId) → {orderId, status}; compensate: cancelOrder(orderId)","coupling_type":"command (sync or async command/reply)"},{"from":"Saga Orchestrator","to":"Inventory Service","contract":"reserveInventory(orderId, items[]) → {reservationId, status}; compensate: releaseInventory(orderId, reservationId)","coupling_type":"command (sync or async command/reply)"},{"from":"Saga Orchestrator","to":"Payment Service","contract":"chargePayment(orderId, customerId, amount) → {paymentId, status}; compensate: refundPayment(orderId, paymentId)","coupling_type":"command (sync or async command/reply)"},{"from":"Saga Orchestrator","to":"Courier Service","contract":"assignCourier(orderId, pickupLocation, deliveryLocation) → {assignmentId, courierId, status}; compensate: unassignCourier(orderId, assignmentId)","coupling_type":"command (sync or async command/reply)"},{"from":"Saga Orchestrator","to":"Saga State Store","contract":"saveSagaState(sagaId, step, status, compensationLog); loadSagaState(sagaId)","coupling_type":"direct (infrastructure dependency)"}],"data_flow":"1. Client submits order → Orchestrator creates saga instance (state: STARTED). 2. Orchestrator calls Order Service → createOrder → on success, saga advances to step 2, records compensating action cancelOrder. 3. Orchestrator calls Inventory Service → reserveInventory → on success, advances to step 3, records compensating action releaseInventory. 4. Orchestrator calls Payment Service → chargePayment → on success, advances to step 4, records compensating action refundPayment. 5. Orchestrator calls Courier Service → assignCourier → on success, saga state = COMPLETED, Order Service notified to set status CONFIRMED. On ANY step failure: Orchestrator sets saga state = COMPENSATING, iterates the compensation log in REVERSE order, executing each compensating action. After all compensations succeed, saga state = COMPENSATED.","patterns_used":[{"pattern":"Orchestration Saga","rationale":"Central orchestrator provides clear visibility into saga state, deterministic step ordering, and straightforward compensation logic. Preferred over choreography here because the flow is strictly sequential with 4 steps—choreography would scatter the flow across services making it hard to reason about compensation order."},{"pattern":"Compensating Transaction","rationale":"Each forward step has a corresponding undo operation. This is the fundamental mechanism for maintaining consistency without distributed transactions."},{"pattern":"Idempotent Operations","rationale":"Every forward and compensating operation must be idempotent (keyed by orderId + step). Network retries and crash recovery will re-deliver commands—idempotency prevents double-charges, double-reservations, etc."},{"pattern":"Saga State Machine","rationale":"Explicit state machine (STARTED → STEP_N_PENDING → STEP_N_COMPLETE → ... → COMPLETED | COMPENSATING → COMPENSATED) makes the saga debuggable, recoverable, and auditable."},{"pattern":"Command/Reply (Async Messaging)","rationale":"Prefer async command channels with reply queues for resilience. Orchestrator sends command, service processes and replies. Decouples availability—services can be temporarily down without losing saga progress."}]},"domain_model":{"bounded_contexts":[{"name":"Order Management","classification":"core","aggregates":["Order (root: orderId, status, items[], customerId, timestamps)"]},{"name":"Inventory","classification":"supporting","aggregates":["Reservation (root: reservationId, orderId, items[], status)"]},{"name":"Payment","classification":"generic","aggregates":["Payment (root: paymentId, orderId, customerId, amount, status)"]},{"name":"Courier Assignment","classification":"core","aggregates":["Assignment (root: assignmentId, orderId, courierId, status)"]},{"name":"Saga Coordination","classification":"supporting","aggregates":["SagaInstance (root: sagaId, orderId, currentStep, status, compensationLog[])"]}],"context_map":[{"upstream":"Saga Coordination","downstream":"Order Management","relationship":"Customer-Supplier: Orchestrator commands Order Service; Order Service conforms to command/reply contract"},{"upstream":"Saga Coordination","downstream":"Inventory","relationship":"Customer-Supplier: Orchestrator commands Inventory Service"},{"upstream":"Saga Coordination","downstream":"Payment","relationship":"Customer-Supplier: Orchestrator commands Payment Service; consider ACL if using external payment gateway"},{"upstream":"Saga Coordination","downstream":"Courier Assignment","relationship":"Customer-Supplier: Orchestrator commands Courier Service"}]},"trade_offs":[{"decision":"Orchestration over Choreography","gains":"Single point of visibility for saga state. Easy to reason about compensation order. Simple to add monitoring, alerting, and manual intervention. Straightforward testing—mock services, assert step sequences.","sacrifices":"Orchestrator is a coordination bottleneck and single point of failure (mitigated by stateless orchestrator + durable state store). Slightly more coupling—orchestrator knows all services.","alternatives_considered":["Choreography saga: each service emits events, next service reacts. Pro: no central coordinator. Con: compensation order becomes implicit and scattered, very hard to debug with 4+ steps, requires careful event routing.","Hybrid: orchestration for happy path, choreography for compensations. Con: unnecessary complexity for a 4-step linear flow."]},{"decision":"Async command/reply over synchronous HTTP calls","gains":"Resilience to transient failures. Natural retry semantics. Services can process at their own pace. No cascading timeouts.","sacrifices":"Higher infrastructure complexity (message broker required). Slightly higher latency for happy path. Harder local development setup.","alternatives_considered":["Synchronous HTTP with retries: simpler to start but cascading failures, timeout management, and partial failure handling become painful at scale."]},{"decision":"Persist saga state before each step","gains":"Crash recovery—orchestrator can restart and resume from last persisted state. Auditability—full history of saga progression.","sacrifices":"Write amplification—one DB write per step transition. Slightly higher latency per step.","alternatives_considered":["In-memory only: faster but saga state lost on crash, requiring manual intervention or restart-from-scratch."]}],"flexibility_points":[{"area":"Adding new saga steps","evolution_path":"The state machine is a list of (step, command, compensation) tuples. Adding step 5 (e.g., Notification Service) means appending to the list. No existing services change."},{"area":"Compensation retry strategies","evolution_path":"Each compensating action can have its own retry policy (exponential backoff, max retries, dead-letter). Policies can evolve independently per service."},{"area":"Sync-to-async migration","evolution_path":"Start with synchronous HTTP for simplicity. Migrate to async messaging per-service without changing the orchestrator's state machine logic—only the transport adapter changes."},{"area":"Parallel steps","evolution_path":"If future requirements allow, steps 2 and 3 (inventory + payment) could execute in parallel. Orchestrator state machine can be extended to support fork/join semantics."}],"rigidity_points":[{"area":"Step ordering","reason":"Business rule mandates sequential execution: order must exist before reserving inventory, inventory must be reserved before charging, payment must succeed before assigning courier. This ordering is a hard constraint."},{"area":"Compensation ordering","reason":"Compensations MUST execute in reverse order of completed steps. This is invariant—violating it causes inconsistent state (e.g., refunding before releasing inventory leaves phantom reservations)."},{"area":"Idempotency requirement","reason":"Every service operation MUST be idempotent. This is non-negotiable for saga correctness. Removing idempotency guarantees breaks crash recovery and retry semantics."}],"risks":[{"risk":"Compensation failure: a compensating action itself fails (e.g., refund fails due to payment provider outage)","severity":"critical","mitigation":"Implement retry with exponential backoff for compensations. After max retries, move to dead-letter queue for manual intervention. Alert operations team. Never silently drop a failed compensation."},{"risk":"Orchestrator crash mid-saga","severity":"high","mitigation":"Persist saga state before each step transition. On restart, orchestrator loads incomplete sagas and resumes from last persisted state. Idempotent operations ensure safe re-execution."},{"risk":"Zombie sagas: sagas that get stuck and never complete or compensate","severity":"high","mitigation":"Implement saga timeout (e.g., 5 minutes). Background sweeper detects stale sagas and triggers compensation. Dashboard for operators to view and manually resolve stuck sagas."},{"risk":"Non-idempotent service implementation causes double-charge or double-reservation","severity":"critical","mitigation":"Enforce idempotency keys (orderId + stepId) at every service boundary. Include idempotency verification in integration tests. Code review checklist item."},{"risk":"Message ordering issues in async mode","severity":"medium","mitigation":"Partition message queues by orderId to guarantee ordering within a saga. Orchestrator processes one command-reply at a time per saga instance."},{"risk":"Saga state store becomes bottleneck under high order volume","severity":"medium","mitigation":"Partition saga state by orderId hash. Use a high-throughput store (e.g., DynamoDB, Cassandra). Saga instances are independent—horizontal scaling is natural."}],"implementation_guidance":{"recommended_approach":"Define the saga as a declarative step list: [{name, command, compensation}]. The orchestrator engine interprets this list generically—advancing on success, reversing on failure. Each service exposes two endpoints per saga step: the forward action and its compensation. Start with synchronous HTTP for simplicity; extract to async messaging when latency or resilience demands it. Persist saga state in a dedicated table with columns: sagaId, orderId, currentStep, status (STARTED|STEP_N_PENDING|COMPENSATING|COMPLETED|COMPENSATED|FAILED), compensationLog (JSON array of completed steps with their undo commands), createdAt, updatedAt.","files_to_create_or_modify":["src/saga/OrderSagaDefinition — declares the 4 steps with forward/compensate commands","src/saga/SagaOrchestrator — generic engine that executes any saga definition","src/saga/SagaStateRepository — persistence for saga instances","src/saga/SagaRecoveryWorker — background job that detects and resumes stuck sagas","src/order-service/handlers/createOrder, cancelOrder — forward and compensating handlers","src/inventory-service/handlers/reserveInventory, releaseInventory","src/payment-service/handlers/chargePayment, refundPayment","src/courier-service/handlers/assignCourier, unassignCourier"],"patterns_to_follow":"Each service handler: (1) check idempotency key, return cached result if duplicate, (2) execute business logic, (3) persist result, (4) return success/failure. The orchestrator: (1) load saga state, (2) execute next step, (3) on success—persist updated state—advance, (4) on failure—persist COMPENSATING status—execute compensations in reverse, (5) on all compensations done—persist COMPENSATED.","pitfalls_to_avoid":["Do NOT use distributed transactions (2PC)—they don't scale and most message brokers/databases don't support them across services","Do NOT skip persisting saga state between steps—crash recovery depends on it","Do NOT assume compensations always succeed—build retry and dead-letter handling from day one","Do NOT make the orchestrator stateful in-memory—it must be stateless and reconstructable from the state store","Do NOT mix saga coordination logic with business logic—keep the orchestrator generic and the business rules in services","Do NOT forget to handle the edge case where createOrder succeeds but the saga orchestrator crashes before recording it—use transactional outbox or write saga state and order atomically if co-located"]},"escalations":[],"confidence":{"level":92,"high_confidence_areas":["Saga pattern selection (orchestration over choreography for linear 4-step flow)","Compensation ordering and idempotency requirements","State machine design and crash recovery approach","Risk identification for distributed saga execution"],"low_confidence_areas":["Specific technology choices (message broker, state store) depend on existing infrastructure which is unspecified","Exact timeout values and retry policies require load testing and SLA definitions","Whether sync or async messaging is right for v1 depends on current team capabilities and infrastructure"],"assumptions":["Services are independently deployable and have their own data stores","A durable storage mechanism (RDBMS or document store) is available for saga state","Network is unreliable—any call between services can fail, timeout, or be duplicated","Each service can implement idempotent operations","Order volume is moderate enough that a single saga state store partition is sufficient initially"]}}

Drives forward steps
